figures.caption: Figure 1: Schematic of: (Left) The Feedfroward Neural Network (Right) The Recurrent Fredholm Neural Network. The recurrent layer applies the non-linearity G(·) pointwise to the output of each node. The grid points zi ∈ Rd can be sampled either uniformly or using quasi-Monte Carlo methods such as Sobol sampling.

Figure 6: Results for Example 5.3: (Top left) The loss function evolution during training. (Top middle) Comparison between the true solution to the integral equation f for a given gtesti in the test set, and the estimated solution f̂(x), as calculated by the forward Fredholm NN with the best (minimum rel. L2 error) learned kernel Kθ. (Top right) The corresponding absolute error plot |f̂(x)−f(x)|. (Bottom left) The sup-norm of the consecutive layers in the Fredholm NN with the learned kernel, showing that the learned integral operator is a contraction and converges to the fixed point solution. (Bottom middle - right) The contour of the learned kernel modelKθ and plot of the non-linearityGϑ (median values used).

Figure 7: Results for Example 5.4: (Top row, from left to right) a) The loss function evolution during training. b) Comparison between the true solution to the integral equation f in the test set, and the estimated solution f̂(x), as calculated by the forward Fredholm NN with the best (minimum rel. L2 error) learned kernel Kθ and non-linearity Gϑ. Again, for illustration we plot the values at each grid node, with the nodes reordered so that f(x) increases monotonically. Here, we use the unseen test grid. c) The plot of the corresponding absolute errors, |f̂(x)− f(x)|. d) The distribution of the absolute errors for the test functions gtest, across all N = 1024 Sobol samples in R10. (Bottom left) The norm of the consecutive layers in the Fredholm NN with the learned kernel, showing the convergence to the fixed point solution. (Bottom middle) The contour of a slice of the learned kernel model Kθ(x, z) with x = (0.5, · · · , 0.5) and z = (z1, 0.5, . . . , 0.5, z10). (Bottom left) The plot of the learned non-linearity Gϑ (median values used).

Figure 4: Results for Example 5.1: (Top left) The loss function evolution during training. (Top right) Comparison between the true solution to the integral equation ftest, for a given gtest in the test set, and the estimated solution f̂test(x), as calculated by the forward Fredholm NN with the best (minimum rel. L2 error) learned kernelKθ. (Bottom left) The sup-norm of the consecutive layers in the Fredholm NN with the learned kernel, showing that the learned integral operator is a contraction and converges to the fixed point solution. (Right) The contour of the learned kernel model Kθ.

Figure 3: The Potential Fredholm Neural Network (PFNN) for the solution of 2D linear elliptic PDEs: the first component is a Fredholm NN solving the Boundary Integral Equation and the final hidden layer corresponds to the double layer potential formulation of the solution to the PDE, ũ(r, ϕ).

Figure 8: Results for Example 5.5: (Top left) The loss function evolution during training. (Top middle) Estimated û(r, θ), as calculated by the forward Potential Fredholm NN with the best (minimum rel. L2 error) learned model Φθ, for a given test boundary function gtest. (Top right) Absolute error between the approximated solution û and the true test data u, for gtest. (Bottom left) The sup-norm of the consecutive layers in the Fredholm NN with the learned kernel, showing that the learned integral operator is a contraction and converges to the fixed point solution. (Right) The contour of the learned kernel model Kθ.
Table 2: Errors for Example 5.5: per-run metrics are calculated over the 20 averages from the 20 test functions. Persample statistics are calculated over all 20× 20 = 400 evaluations.

Figure 5: Results for Example 5.2: (Top left) The loss function evolution during training. (Top middle) Comparison between the true solution to the integral equation f in the test set, and the estimated solution f̂(x), as calculated by the forward Fredholm NN with the best (minimum rel. L2 error) learned kernel Kθ. For the illustration we plot the values at each grid node, with the nodes reordered so that f(x) increases monotonically. (Top right) The plot of the corresponding absolute errors, |f̂(x) − f(x)|, at each grid node. (Bottom left) The distribution of the absolute errors for the test functions gtest, across all N = 1024 Sobol samples in R10. (Bottom middle) The norm of the consecutive layers in the Fredholm NN with the learned kernel, showing the convergence to the fixed point solution. (Bottom right) The contour of a slice of the learned kernel model Kθ(x, z) with x = (0.5, · · · , 0.5) and z = (z1, 0.5, . . . , 0.5, z10).

Figure 2: The Fredholm Neural Operator (FREDINO) framework: The training data consists of a family of functions gi and the corresponding solutions to the FIE fi, for i = 1, . . . , n. We model the unknown integral kernel and the non-linearity with two neural networks Kθ(x, y) and Gϑ(x). These models then populate the parameters of the Fredholm Neural Network. The loss function L(Θ) is calculated by comparing output of the populated Fredholm NN, f̂i, i = 1, . . . , n with the training data. For brevity, the schematic refers to the case of the non-linear FIE, using the Recurrent Fredholm NN. The linear case is simpler, where we only consider Kθ.
